title: Born at a Very Early Age
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project in Philadelphia on July 15, 2011, John Churchville opens by recalling that he was born with an extreme case of myopia and attended a sight-saving program, where in the fifth grade he first faced discrimination not for being Black but for being a sight-saver.

title: The Site-Savers Had Their Own Lunchroom
summary: Churchville describes the internal segregation at T.M. Pierce School in North Philadelphia, where sight-savers had their own lunchroom and safety patrol and could only direct other sight-savers, and how a teacher urged him to run for student council to raise the issue.

title: Gee Whiz, Was I Bullied
topic: The Bully and a Ukrainian Friend
summary: He recounts forgetting his identity once elected student council president, learning the lesson only later, and then being beaten daily by a bully in seventh grade until a friend referred him to a Ukrainian classmate, a self-described Nazi who could quote Mein Kampf yet whose favorite teacher and best friend were not who one would expect.

title: He Brought Me a Zip Gun
topic: The Day He Almost Killed His Tormentor
summary: Churchville tells how the Ukrainian classmate built him a zip gun with twenty-two caliber bullets, how he carried it to school intending to end his tormentor's life, and how the thought of disappointing his mother stopped him at the last moment; the bully beat him lightly that day and never bothered him again, and was killed in a gang six months later.

title: How's Your Mommy
summary: He reflects that the bully was Black, recalls a German teacher's loaded question about his mother that his own mother taught him to answer in kind, and contrasts supportive teachers like Mrs. Maines and Mrs. Corn with counselors who told Black students to go to trade school and forget college.

title: He Taught Us the Stock Market
summary: Churchville credits a ninth-grade social studies teacher who, though blunt that Black students should not expect good jobs, made them follow stocks daily and taught that wealth in the United States comes not from a nine-to-five job but from investing, a lesson he still appreciates.

title: Always Examine Your Motives
summary: He describes his mother, a well-read musician with a non-traditional mind who never made excuses for injustice, who became his best friend and taught him to always examine his motives and know why he was doing whatever he did.

title: The Village Concert Choir
topic: His Mother's Music in North Philadelphia
summary: Churchville recounts his mother turning down an operatic opportunity to raise her children, running the Village Concert Choir that sang Bach, Mozart, and Negro spirituals on summer nights in North Philadelphia, and how he learned to fake his way through her piano students' voice lessons by memorizing the right chords and notes.

title: I Won a Music Scholarship
summary: He describes directing the Simon Gratz High School choir when its director Joy Going Smith ran late, staging Gilbert and Sullivan's Trial by Jury with teenagers in Germantown, and winning a music scholarship to Temple University on the strength of a great ear despite being too near-sighted to read the notes.

title: I Kwituated From Temple
topic: Moving to the Lower East Side
summary: Churchville tells how at nineteen he left Temple after a year and a half and moved to New York's Lower East Side, an address on East Houston Street, where he encountered Marxists, poets, musicians, artists, and Black nationalists, listened to poetry at the Pink Pussycat, and played some jazz piano.

title: Whatever Gets You to Take a Bath
summary: He recalls debating his mother that the church was gutless and irrelevant, her steadfast love of Jesus, and how at seventeen or eighteen he became an Ahmadiyya Muslim after meeting a sharp young man who taught him Black history and Islamic history, including that the Moors who built the culture of Spain were Black; his Muslim name was Yahya Abdul Hamid.

title: Sure, I'll Take a Ride
summary: Churchville describes working as a janitor at Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center while five non-paying friends crowded his rent-controlled apartment, and how, jobless and at loose ends, he agreed to ride a bus that needed delivering to a SNCC retreat in Georgia purely as a way to take a ride.

title: Under Sherrod's Wing
topic: The Albany Project and Malcolm X
summary: He recounts being impressed by the young people in Georgia talking about freedom and justice, hooking up with Reverend Charles Sherrod who ran the Albany Project, working in Lee County, and an earlier New York encounter outside Micheaux's bookstore where Malcolm X told him his own life was irrelevant and what mattered was Black people getting their act together.

title: I Need a Black Partner
summary: Churchville explains that as a Black nationalist he found it intolerable that Black people deferred to his white partner as the power symbol, so he asked Sherrod for a Black partner and accepted Sherrod's deal that he could keep one only if he registered more voters, then went to work in Americus.

title: Stop Fighting Each Other
summary: He tells how he walked into the middle of a gang fight in Americus, Georgia and got two teenage Black gangs to stop and negotiate a peace treaty, framing voting not as integration but as building a power base to elect people who look like you, the beginning of what he called Black power.

title: Fear Will Get You Up
topic: Violence and Faith in Southwest Georgia
summary: Churchville describes the terrible violence of Southwest Georgia, blaming his high blood pressure on the fear, and how, surrounded by frightened committed people, everyone started singing; though a Muslim, he prayed to Jesus and once castigated a deacon for an insincere prayer while the Klan threatened to burn the church.

title: Cleaning Up After His Mess
summary: He admits that, picking up SNCC's resentment, he thought Martin Luther King was too light and not on point, blamed him for leaving Albany when the cameras left while SNCC stayed, and held issues over King's role with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, realizing only at King's death how much he respected him.

title: Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around
summary: Churchville recalls the Monday-night mass meetings in Albany and a preacher who came bearing a state travel letter to tell them to cease and desist, how the crowd, led by Charlie Sherrod's watching, grew suspicious and drowned him out singing Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around until he sat down.

title: The Older Folks Had the Guts
topic: A Sheriff and a Family's Helplessness
summary: He observes that older grandmothers, not the young people, took the risks for change, and recounts a wrenching case where a notorious sheriff held a gun to a boy's head, threatening to kill him or to claim he molested a white girl after a bakery driver had raped the boy's sister, leaving Churchville with rage he had nowhere to put.

title: A Yellow Streak All the Way Down
topic: Why He Stayed Despite the Fear
summary: Churchville confesses he was scared enough to run the first day but stayed because the local people had far more to lose and could not leave, recalling the FBI agents who came down, did nothing, and reported back to the sheriff whatever was said, and the daily atrocities that never came to light.

part: Mississippi and Living in the Moment
title: I Chose the Voter Registration Camp
summary: He explains the two strains in SNCC, direct action and voter registration, and why he chose voter registration as a way out he could believe in, refusing to be beaten over a lunch counter, appalled that Black citizens had to pass a constitutional test, and resolving simply to do what was in front of him.

title: The Privilege of the Moment
summary: Churchville describes moving to Mississippi to teach literacy at the COFO Freedom School in Greenwood rather than getting people to the polls, calling it the first time in his life he lived fully in the moment, an experience that later drew him toward teaching when he returned to Philadelphia.

title: The Sweetheart of the Movement
summary: He names the people who stand out in memory from fifty years before: Charles Sherrod, Diane Nash, Bernice Johnson Reagon who brought song and more to the movement, Faith Holsaert and Bob Mants in his camp, community folk like Eddie, and Dorie Ann Ladner.

title: Justice, Not Integration
topic: Respect and Disagreement With John Lewis
summary: Churchville voices deep respect for John Lewis, who said the same thing asleep, awake, or before cameras and never came back at a critic, while disagreeing with his vision of integration; Churchville drew a category line between integration as a personal choice and economic justice as a necessity.

part: The Nation of Islam in Atlanta
title: Buttermilk Bottom
summary: He recounts leaving SNCC, joining the Nation of Islam in Atlanta under Minister Jeremiah Pugh, trying to recruit SNCC people to the mosque, and organizing the very poor unpaved community of Buttermilk Bottom on the theory that Black people should develop themselves rather than be nice to white folks.

title: The Talented Tenth
summary: Churchville describes being turned down by the Nation's Chicago headquarters when he sought permission to organize young people to sell papers, his W.E.B. Du Bois-inspired effort to recruit a talented tenth of Atlanta University professionals including a mathematician and an economist, and Chicago shooting down their proposal too, which first made him feel the Nation was not what it advertised.

title: He Got Sent to Philadelphia
summary: He tells how, losing interest in the Black Muslim movement, he followed Jeremiah Pugh back to Philadelphia on a promise to be made secretary of the mosque, a promise Elijah Muhammad overruled, and recalls Pugh's honesty in once telling him to get a grip about Elijah Muhammad being just a guy.

title: The Freedom Library
topic: Founding the Northern Student Movement Library
summary: Churchville recounts joining the Northern Student Movement and opening the Freedom Library on Ridge Avenue, a storefront of books by and about Black people built on his belief that low self-esteem and ignorance of history held Black people back, which survived a North Philadelphia riot untouched and won a Dolfinger-McMahon Foundation grant.

part: Malcolm X, Christianity, and the FBI
title: Don't Touch Him
summary: He describes meeting Malcolm X in New York after Malcolm left the Nation, agreeing to help start a Philadelphia chapter, joining the Organization of Afro-American Unity, and how putting Malcolm's picture in the Freedom Library window led Jeremiah Pugh to order the Fruit of Islam not to touch him and to beg his mother to intervene.

title: I Just Preached Jesus
topic: Conversion and a Debate With Pugh
summary: Churchville recounts converting to Christianity around 1966 through a personal encounter in his own house, then debating Minister Jeremiah Pugh publicly with the Fruit of Islam standing by, praising what he had learned from Elijah Muhammad while testifying that as a Muslim he had lacked an internal witness that God was with him, and Pugh's joke that this first Muslim convert to Christianity would be the last and his private admission that, had he known Jesus as Churchville described him, he never would have trashed him.

title: I Was Devastated
summary: Churchville describes being devastated by Malcolm X's killing in 1965, reflecting on how people brought leaders criticism instead of help, and how the same people who said they would follow Malcolm if only he left the Nation still would not take a risk once he did, teaching him about human nature and fear.

title: I Was In It to Win It
summary: He explains his conviction that taking a principled stand, win or lose, lets him sleep at night because history eventually comes back around, then turns to the FBI coming to him at the Freedom Library seeking information about James Forman's reparations work, which he refused.

title: On the Pink List
summary: Churchville recounts being told he was on the pink list as both a Muslim and a communist when seeking work, learning the FBI had warned Southern contacts he was coming to foment violence, and his amusement that he never owned or advocated a gun, framing his view instead as creative versus destructive violence.

title: A Faulty but Energizing Analysis
topic: Why a Library Became a School
summary: He describes seeing in SNCC and the Atlanta mosque how Black history energized people who felt stuck, and building the Freedom Library on Ridge Avenue near high-rise projects on the analysis, which he now calls fallacious, that Black people's central problem was not knowing their history.

title: Everybody Knows Your Name
summary: Churchville recounts Wednesday-night history classes where patrons from the bar next door would chime in and correct his dates, teaching him before Cheers ever aired why people love bars, and reshaping his sense of his role from solving problems to eliciting and empowering what people already knew.

title: Three Steps Back
summary: He tells how stranded tutors left him teaching children who could not grasp their homework because they had missed fundamentals three steps back, leading him to start with preschoolers not yet corrupted by the education system, and how a teacher using the look-say method soon gave up.

title: I Found a Miracle
summary: Churchville describes recalling first-grade phonetics with Mrs. Freed, discovering the J.B. Lippincott reading series that had children reading two years ahead, and using his own sons as guinea pigs, with his youngest son David reading at about a year and a half.

title: Like the Pied Piper
topic: Opening the School for Ten Dollars a Week
summary: He recounts going through the neighborhood like the Pied Piper recruiting children two and a half to five years old, incorporating Freedom Library Day School, charging ten dollars a week with lunch included, praying for ten students and starting with three, and using the individual prescriptive method to let each child move at their own pace.

title: I Enjoyed It and I Hated It
summary: Churchville describes grouping children so they pushed each other, finding his methods worked though teachers called them horrendous, and the exhausting nightly labor of grading forty-five separate homework books and preparing each child's next individual lesson, work he hated even as he loved watching them learn.

title: Skills and Blackness Both
summary: He insists the work was simply what the children needed, not radical posturing, and contrasts his emphasis on skills, character, and history with other nationalist schools heavy on rhetoric and drums, naming his disagreement with Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga for building an ideological base without giving children usable skills.

title: How to Make a Blackboard
summary: Churchville recounts helping form CIBI in the early 1970s and running a two-week teacher-training institute at the Freedom Library Day School that taught teachers in independent Black schools how to build blackboards and room dividers, recruit children door to door, and demand the same work from a child labeled learning disabled as from any other, with lasting results.

title: Room to Voice Our Nationalism
topic: Father Paul Washington and the Gangs
summary: He describes coming to know Father Paul M. Washington, the North Philadelphia priest who gave them room to voice their nationalism when other churches found them tainted, and taking a small salary from a donor to work with gangs, where he began to see gang members as potential community leaders.

title: Gang Warring Is My Life
summary: Churchville recounts a gang member telling him that gang warring was all he had, sending him back to imagine community protection as an alternative role, and his confrontation with the donor and with Father Washington when the funder cut the money for steering the work toward the gang members' real interests.

title: We Tried to Make It Inclusive
summary: He explains that the Black People's Unity Movement differed from others by being inclusive of gang members, elders, professionals, and artists like Arthur Hall and John E. Allen Jr. and future state representative David P. Richardson, tolerating fourteen points of view among seven people and seeking only what all could agree on.

title: The Infamous Dynamite Case
topic: A Police Plant and a Setup
summary: Churchville recalls the founding principles that white supremacy was constitutive of United States culture and that Black progress depended on intraracial resources, then turns to the SNCC Dynamite case, describing Casamiro, a police plant who proposed bringing M-16s to the school, and his belief that the man arrested was set up with planted dynamite.

title: They Broke Down the Door at One A.M.
topic: Rizzo's Raid on the Freedom Library
summary: He describes police commissioner Rizzo's officers breaking down the Freedom Library door around one in the morning in 1966 in bulletproof vests with sawed-off shotguns while his pregnant wife was horrified, how he calmly asked them to search the bedroom first so he could shelter her, and refused to give them the resisting-arrest pretext they sought.

title: They Had a Warrant
summary: Churchville recounts that the terror only struck after the police left and he could not sleep for nights, that they had been looking for dynamite, and that his lawyer and Black People's Unity Movement member William Lee Akers told him they had a warrant and nothing could be done, a hard moment given that he had never owned a gun or preached killing.

title: Don't Get It Confused
summary: He describes dispersing a crowd outside that was ready to confront the police, lest they be mowed down, and deciding not to let his violated pride derail his work, framing the August 13, 1966 raid by FBI and Philadelphia police as a massive show of force by the white police establishment against the SNCC office and Freedom Library.

title: I Function With Blinders
summary: Churchville explains that he disciplined himself to stay in his lane and not get involved in the 1967 high school student organizing or the violence that befell those children, declining to chase press or causes outside his chosen work because his mother taught him to examine his motives.

title: How Do We Get Rid of You
topic: Voted Out as a Jesus Chairman
summary: He recounts continuing the school until 1978, telling the Black People's Unity Movement around 1966 that as chairman he would use Jesus's leadership principles, being voted out when a member asked how to remove him, and feeling vindicated as friends called him a Jesus freak while he came to see Jesus as a revolutionary.

title: I Was Very Anti-Politics
topic: Refusing the War on Poverty Money
summary: Churchville describes staying the course as people dropped away, turning history nights into Bible study, and his deep aversion to electoral politics and to the war-on-poverty funding streams that he felt turned self-styled community leaders into people chasing money, choosing instead to do his work without asking anyone for funds.

title: When Things Got Frustrating, I Did National Stuff
topic: CIBI Curriculum and a CBS Documentary
summary: He recounts turning to national work when local efforts frustrated him, developing CIBI curriculum and trainings, and how a CBS documentary aired around 1969 led to speaking at colleges and demonstration teaching with children in Chicago, where he insisted no one should heed people who talk about teaching children but do not teach children.

title: Revolutionary Versus Cultural Nationalism
summary: Churchville frames a strong period of struggle between revolutionary nationalism and the cultural nationalism of California and Newark, arguing for training community-trusted people and electing them outside the corrupt political machine, and describes letting a stalled project sit while he worked another so as never to be discouraged.

title: I Was Supposed to Die
topic: A Car Accident and a Turn to Theology
summary: He describes a serious car accident around 1995 that he survived against expectation, which refocused him and drew him to study theology, and his realization that practicing law was not fun, beginning as a farm-worker attorney in Cumberland County, New Jersey, work that recalled the danger of Mississippi and Georgia.

title: Practical Sound Advice
topic: From Criminal Law to a Leadership Institute
summary: Churchville recounts moving to Philadelphia to practice estate planning and criminal law, growing frustrated trying to redirect the young people he defended, and after the accident developing the Churchville Leadership Institute to translate the practical wisdom of Jesus, Moses, and the prophets into sound advice without naming it religious.

title: Just Get My Piece of Paper
summary: He describes attending Lutheran Theological Seminary at Philadelphia and being disturbed by fellow students aiming to be noticed by a bishop rather than serving a community, then pursuing African American studies at the Union Institute and University to deepen his theology and history.

title: The Gospel to Empower or to Keep Down
topic: A Thesis on Religion and Liberation
summary: Churchville explains his thesis on whether religious thought makes people quiet or self-liberative, finding cases on both sides, the gospel used as in SNCC to empower people to fight and used in mining towns to keep miners from revolting, and his discouraging year teaching at the seminary that taught him people in a mill just want their paper.

part: Liberation Fellowship and the Town Hall
title: A Non-Religious Church
summary: He recounts starting Liberation Fellowship Church of Jesus around 2005 in Germantown, a church framed around practical leadership lessons drawn from the Bible with a question-and-answer period and open Bible study, drawing Muslims, atheists, and others, out of which the small congregation built a community development corporation everyone helped write.

title: What Are We Going to Do With This Thing
topic: HIV Education and a Business Association
summary: Churchville describes the CDC's HIV/AIDS community education program, for which he and his wife Nancy became certified testers and counselors and ran a health fair in Vernon Park, and his founding of the Greater Germantown Business Association after an earlier association deteriorated under real estate disputes.

title: Make Everybody Feel at Home
topic: A Diverse Association and Town Hall
summary: He recounts going store to store to build a diverse business association reflecting Germantown's Arab, Muslim, African, Asian, and Korean communities, and the Liberation Fellowship CDC taking on the redevelopment of the historic Germantown Town Hall at 5928 Germantown Avenue, near the birthplace of the antislavery movement.

title: You Got Thirty-Five Million Dollars
topic: A Vision for the Town Hall
summary: Churchville describes the building lying in disrepair since the mid-1990s, a councilwoman laughing off the cost when he proposed acquiring it around 2008, and his vision, now taken seriously by the mayor's office of sustainability, of a green building using geothermal and solar power that houses dual education.

title: A Rival to MIT in Germantown
summary: He lays out the plan for the Town Hall as a rival to MIT teaching STEM subjects, green job training in plumbing and welding across from Germantown High School, and a green business incubator, ties it to the 2011 Brownfields Conference, and closes the interview with thanks from the museum and the library.
